Facts.text: St Julius I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Riots in Lynn, Norfolk (England) spread to Norwich
King Henri de Bourbon of Navarra becomes leader of the Huguenots
Huguenot rebels and the French sign the Peace of La Rochelle
Shah Jahan ascends to the Mughal throne in Agra, after securing it by ordering the murder of his half brother and nephews
French Chief Minister Cardinal Mazarin flees Paris
Britain & Netherlands renew alliance
Niccolò Piccinni's opera buffa "La buona figliuola" (The Good-Natured Girl) first performed at the Teatro delle Dame, Rome, Italy, with an all-male cast

Treaty of Amity and Commerce and the Treaty of Alliance are signed in Paris between the United States and France, the first treaties negotiated by the United States. The first treaty establishes formal diplomatic and commercial relations, and the second forms a defensive alliance.
Massachusetts becomes 6th state to ratify constitution
New Jersey issues 1st US railroad charter (John Stevens)
First 86 African American émigrés sponsored by the American Colonization Society set sail from New York City to Sierra Leone to start a settlement (present-day Liberia)
US population announced at 9,638,453, African Americans 1,771,656 (18.4%)
1st appearance of cholera in Edinburgh, Scotland
US ship destroys Sumatran village in retaliation for piracy
First minstrel show in the United States, "The Virginia Minstrels," opens at the Bowery Amphitheatre in NYC
1st meeting of Provisional Congress of Confederate States of America (US Civil War)
British Vice-Admiral Robert Fitzroy issues first storm warnings for ships
American Civil War: Naval engagement on Tennessee River, USS Conestago vs CSS Appleton Belle
-7] Skirmish at Barnett's Ford Virginia
US Civil War: Second day of battle at Dabney's Mills (Hatcher's Run)
American financier and philanthropist George Peabody establishes the Peabody Education Fund to provide improvements to existing schools in poor areas of the southern USA
Harper's Weekly publishes the first picture of Uncle Sam with chin whiskers
The society of the Knights of Columbus forms in New Haven, Connecticut
1st great train robbery by the Dalton Gang: Southern Pacific #17, near Alila (now Earlimart), California
Bottle opener patented by American inventor William Painter
Concert debut of Romanian composerGeorge Enescu(16), as the Orchestre de Édouard Colonne premieres his "Poema Română" in Paris, France
The Battle of Vaal Krantz, South Africa (Boers vs British army)
Young Women's Hebrew Association organizes in NYC
Japan notifies Russia that in view of Russia's delaying tactics and provocative military action, Japan is ending negotiations and recalling its members from Moscow
First old-age home opens in Prescott, Arizona
Great fire destroys downtown Constantinople (Istanbul, Turkey)
Great Britain grants women aged 30 and over the right to vote
First day of the five-day Seattle General Strike
The first day of the Weimar Republic which, because of its support of the Treaty of Versailles, does not receive proper allegiance from the German nation
Saarland administrated by League of Nations
US, UK, France, Italy, and Japan sign the Washington Naval Arms Limitation Treaty
MLB St Louis Browns acquire catcher Wally Schang from New York Yankees for cash and pitcher George Mogridge
NFL rules college students are ineligible until they complete their college careers
A woman dubbed Anna Anderson [possibly Franziska Schanzkowska] arrives in NYC, using the alias "Anastasia Tschaikovsky" claims to be Grand Duchess Anastasia, daughter of Tsar Nicholas II
Fascist coup in the Memel territory
First Olympic dog sled race is held at Lake Placid, New York, as a demonstration sport
-90°F (-68°C), Oymyakon, USSR (Asian record)
Highest recorded sea wave (not tsunami), 34 m, in North Pacific hurricane by USS Ramapo
Far right leagues rally in front of the Palais Bourbon in an attempted coup against the French Third Republic, creating a political crisis in France
MLB Cincinnati Reds purchase contract of 43-year-old pitcher Dazzy Vance from St. Louis Cardinals for $7,500
Board game Monopoly goes on sale for the first time
First election allowing women to vote in Turkey
IV Winter Olympic Games open in Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany
K Elizabeth Ohi becomes 1st Japanese-US female lawyer
Spanish government flees to France
Auke Adema wins 6th official Dutch 11 Cities Skating Race (9:19)
Battle of Beda Fomm: Italian 10th army destroyed
British troops conquer Bengazi, Libya
1st Spitfire in action above Darwin, Australia, Mu Ki-46 shot down
WWII: Russian Red Army crosses the Oder River between Poland and Germany
WWII: US 8th Air Force bombs oil facilities in Magdeburg and Chemnitz, Germany
1st radio-controlled airplane flown
KNXT (now KCBS) TV channel 2 in Los Angeles, CA (CBS) 1st broadcast
Nils Karlsson wins the 50k cross country gold medal at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; Swedish skiers win all 3 cross-country events at the Games
World & European champion Barbara Ann Scott becomes the first Canadian to win a women's figure skating Olympic gold medal at the St. Moritz Winter Games
"Broker Special" train crashes in Woodbridge NJ, killing 84
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site Argonne Atomic Lab (Ill), to demonstrate lax in security
Ian Craig makes Test Cricket debut at 17 yrs 239 days, youngest Aussie
US controls on wages & some consumer goods lifted
Chicago's Daily Defender, begins publishing
University of Alabama suspends African-American student Autherine Lucy claiming that it can no longer provide for her safety
21 dead in air crash at Munich-Riem Airport; 8 players and 3 staff are from the Manchester United football team
1st successful test-fire of Titan ICBM
"Jail, No Bail" Jail-in movement starts in Rock Hill, South Carolina
KOAP TV channel 10 in Portland, OR (PBS) begins broadcasting
Schoolman Athletic Field in the Bronx named
WCIU TV channel 26 in Chicago, IL (IND) begins broadcasting
"Kelly" opens & closes at Broadhurst Theater NYC
Righteous Brothers' music single "You've Lost That Lovin' Feelin'" hits #1
Cultural Revolution in Albania
Dutch 2nd Chamber condemns US bombing of North Vietnam
KESD TV channel 8 in Brookings, SD (PBS) begins broadcasting
X Winter Olympic Games opens in Grenoble, France

A&M Records releases "The Gilded Palace of Sin", the debut studio album by American country-rock band The Flying Burrito Brothers; Gram Parsons and Chris Hillman's fusion of modern country,  psychedelic rock, and folk music was a favorite of critics, but not a commercial success
Graeme Pollock completes 274 v Australia at Durban
NBA expands to 18 teams with Buffalo, Cleveland, Houston & Portland
Bernard Watt (28), a Catholic civilian, is shot and killed by the British Army (BA) during street disturbances in Ardoyne, Belfast
James Saunders (22), a member of the IRA, is shot and killed by the British Army during a gun battle near the Oldpark Road, Belfast
The Irish Republican Army shoots and kills Gunner Robert Curtis, the first British soldier to die during the 'Troubles'
A Civil Rights march held in Newry, County Down; very large turn-out with many people attending to protest at the killings in Derry the previous Sunday
40,000 civil servants demonstrate at Binnenhof in The Hague, Netherlands, against higher pension contribution
Bernice Fekete skips her curling rink to 2nd straight 8-ender, Edmonton, Alberta
3rd time Rangers shut-out Islanders 6-0
Dutch speed limit set at 100km due to oil crisis
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
4th time New York Rangers shut-out New York Islanders, 4-0
Alain Prieur jumps his motorcycle 65 m over 16 buses, near Paris
Harley Race beats Terry Funk in Toronto, Ontario to become NWA wrestling champ
Snowstorm hits New England, parts of Rhode Island (54" / 137cm)
Pakistan Supreme Court affirms the Lahore High Court's death sentence against former Former Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto
Suleiman Nyambui runs world record 5k indoor (13:20.4)
TV sitcom "The Brady Brides", a sequel to "The Brady Brunch" debuts on NBC TV, runs for 10 episodes
"Centerfold" by J. Geils Band hits #1 on pop chart
Muslim militiamen take over West Beirut from Lebanese army
No-smoking rules take effect in US federal buildings
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Groundbreaking begins on Baltimore Orioles' new $102 million stadium
Steve Briers of Wales recites entire lyrics of Queen's album "A Night At The Opera" in 9 minutes 58.44 seconds backwards!
Mousey Davis becomes the first coach of the NY/NJ Knights
"Late Night's 10th Anniversary Show At Radio City Music Hall" on NBC
The Sámi people of the far northern Nordic countries have an official day to celebrate their culture
Jose Maria Figueres elected President of Costa Rica
Martti Ahtisaari elected President of Finland
Diane Blood, 32, in England, won right to use her dead husband's sperm
Former teacher Mary Kay LeTourneau (36) who violated probation by seeing the 14-year-old father of her baby, sentenced to 7 years
French Prefect Claude Érignac is assassinated by Yvan Colonna in Ajaccio, Corsica
Sierra Leone military junta is removed, and President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah is restored to office after 9 months in exile
Twins trade Chuck Knoblauch to the NY Yankees for $3 million and four minor-league players
Super Bowl XXXIX, Alltel Stadium, Jacksonville, FL: New England Patriots beat Philadelphia Eagles, 24-21; MVP: Deion Branch, New England, WR
6.9 magnitude quake hits near the central Philippines with 43 confirmed deaths
Queen Elizabeth IImarks the 60th anniversary of becoming British monarch, becoming only the second to do so
9 people are killed as an 8.0 magnitude 8.0 earthquake strikes off the Solomon Islands coast
The Taliban and Pakistani government begin peace negotiations
Eighth Republican presidential candidates debate hosted by ABC, held in Goffstown, New Hampshire
Qatar Airways achieves the longest-ever scheduled commercial flight when its B777 aircraft lands in Auckland after a 16 hour and 23 minutes flight from Doha; surpassed in 2020
6.0 magnitude earthquake strikes Hualien County, Taiwan, leaving 10 dead and over 50 missing
Brazilian jockey Jorge Ricardo equals the world record for the number of victories by a jockey with 12,844 in Rio de Janeiro
Maldives President Abdulla Yameen declares state of emergency ordering arrest of two judges
Polish president Andrzej Duda signs controversial Holocaust law, outlaws accusing Poles of complicity under Nazis
UN calls for ceasefire in rebel-held Eastern Ghouta area by Syrian government troops after 40 people reportedly killed
2018 named the 4th warmest year on record, at the time, according to NOAA and NASA, ranking behind 2016, 2015, and 2017
Honeybees are able to add and subtract and understand concept of zero according to research from RMIT University in Australia
Quadriga, Canada's biggest cryptocurrency exchange is unable to get to $145 million of bitcoin assets after its CEO dies with its access passwords
Antarctica records high temperature of 64.9 F / 18.2 C at Esperanza, Argentina’s research station
Date of the 1st COVID-19 related death in the US (confirmed by the CDC April 21)
US astronaut Christina Koch completes the longest continuous spaceflight by a female astronaut after 328 days on the International Space Station, landing in Kazakhstan
Ottawa Mayor Jim Watson declares a state of emergency over a trucker protest against a covid vaccination mandate on the US-Canada border
Queen Elizabeth IIbecomes the first British monarch to celebrate a Platinum Jubilee, marking seventy years on the throne

Powerful earthquakes strike Turkey and Syria, first with a 7.8 magnitude at 4:15 am, 23 kilometers east of Nurdagi, and second with a 7.5 magnitude at 1:24 pm, with many strong aftershocks causing buildings to collapse. The death toll is 53,537 in Turkey and 8,000 in Syria
'Godfathers of Wind' Henrik Stiesdal and Andrew Garrad awarded QEPrize, the 'Noble of Engineering' for pioneering wind energy
Jennifer Crumbley is the first American parent of a school shooter  to be found guilty of manslaughter, held responsible for her teenager killing four at Oxford High School (2021)
Landslide kills at least 68 with more missing, amid torrential rain, in Maco town, Davao de Oro, southern Philippines
Swedish automaker Polestar introduces its Polestar 4 - first mass-market car with no rear window, an electric SUV coupé with a rear-mounted camera instead
Prince Rahim Al-Hussaini is named Aga Khan V, spiritual leader of millions of Shia Ismaili Muslims in the will of his father Aga Khan IV
US PresidentDonald Trumpreiterates that Gaza could be developed into a "Riviera of the Middle East" by the US, with its population moved elsewhere
Maximilian Iis proclaimed Holy Roman Emperor, the first emperor in centuries not crowned by the Pope
Duke of York becomes KingJames IIof England and VII of Scotland upon the death of his brother Charles II
Stamford Rafflesfounds Singapore as a British trading post
HMS Beagle andCharles Darwinarrive in Van Diemen's Land (Tasmania)
Robert Schumann's 3rd Symphony "Rhenisch" premieres in Dusseldorf
British government ofHenry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston forms
GeneralUlysses S. Grantcaptures Fort Henry in Tennessee
GeneralRobert E. Leeis appointed General-in-Chief of the Confederate Armies during the US Civil War
Charlie Chaplinreleases his first full-length feature, "The Kid," a silent film starring Charlie Chaplin and 6-year-old Jackie Coogan
Cardinal Achille Ratti elected PopePius XI
Rudy Valleerecords single "Deep Night"
German PresidentPaul von HindenburgandFranz von Papenend the Prussian parliament
Pravda criticizesDmitri Shostakovich's ballet "Clear Brook"
SingerFrank Sinatradebuts on radio's "Your Hit Parade"
Magnum Photos founded in Paris byRobert Capa,Henri Cartier-Bresson, George Rodger and David Seymour
Radio commentatorPaul Harveyarrested for trying to sneak into Argonne National Laboratory, a nuclear test site located 20 miles (32 km) west of Chicago, Illinois
Queen Elizabeth IIsucceeds KingGeorge VIto the British throne and is proclaimed Queen of the United Kingdom and the other Commonwealth realms, including Canada, Australia, and New Zealand
French Prime MinisterGuy Molletpelted with tomatoes in Algiers
Future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielderTed Williamsbecomes highest paid player in MLB when he re-signs with Boston Red Sox for $135,000
Edward R. Morrow interviews Cuban revolution leaderFidel Castroon his "Person to Person" program
Texas Instruments files for a patent for it's first integrated circuit (IC), invented byJack Kilby
Muhammad Alipummels Ernie Terrell for 15 rounds to retain his world heavyweight boxing crown at Houston Astrodome; regains Terrell’s WBA belt as well
Former PresidentDwight Eisenhowershoots a hole-in-one
The New Ulster Movement forms, promoting moderate and non-sectarian policies and to assist those candidates who support Northern Ireland Prime MinisterTerence O'Neill
American astronautAlan Shepardis the first to hit a golf ball on the Moon
Dutch speed skaterArd Schenkfollows his win in the 5,000m with victory in the 1,500m, on his way to 3 gold medals at the Sapporo Winter Olympics; also wins 10,000m
American jazz drummerBuddy Richplays influential performance at the Top of the Plaza in Richmond, New York, later televised by PBS
US House of Reps begins determining grounds for impeachment of PresidentRichard Nixon
George Harrisonreleases single "This Guitar (Can't Keep From Crying)"
Muriel Humphrey, former Second Lady, is appointed to serve remainder of late husbandHubert Humphrey's term as US Senator by Minnesota Governor Rudy Perpich
John Wayne Gacygoes on trial for the murder of 33 young men in Cook County, Illinois
BeatlesGeorge Harrison&Paul McCartneyrecord vocals to aRingo Starrtrack written by George, and re-worked as a tribute toJohn Lennon- "All Those Years Ago"
Crime film "Fort Apache: The Bronx" starringPaul Newmanreleased amid protests in the US
Poland's communist government begins "Round Table" talks with the Solidarity trade union and its leaderLech Wałęsain an attempt to ease growing social unrest
Brett Hullbecomes 1st son of NHL 50 goal scorer (Bobby Hull) to also score 50 goals
Riddick BoweTKOs Michael Dokes in the first round for the heavyweight boxing title
MLB outfielderDarryl Strawberrysuspended from baseball for 60 days for positive cocaine test
Columbia Records releases "Tennessee Moon", the twenty-third studio album byNeil Diamond; the record features original collaborations with a variety of country music songwriters and performers
Washington National Airport is renamedRonald ReaganNational Airport
American rapper50 Centreleases his album "Get Rich or Die Trying" (2003 Billboard Album of the Year)
Tony Blair, now the longest-serving Labour PM, marks 2,838 days as British Prime Minister
"Violin Concerto", byJennifer Higdonpremieres, in Indianapolis, Indiana (Pulitzer Prize for Music 2010)
PresidentBarack Obamaannounces the Economic Recovery Advisory Board, withPaul Volckeras Chairman and Austan Goolsbee as Staff Director and Chief Economist
Super Bowl XLV, Cowboys Stadium, Arlington, TX: Green Bay Packers beat Pittsburgh Steelers, 31-25; MVP:Aaron Rodgers, Green Bay, QB
Canadian singerGordon Lightfootis presented with the Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal by the Lieutenant Governor of Ontario
Laureus World Sports Awards, Central Hall Westminster, London, England: Sportsman:Novak Đoković; Sportswoman: Vivian Cheruiyot; Team: FC Barcelona
Jay Lenohosts "The Tonight Show" for the final time (NBC)
Elon Musk's companySpaceXlaunches Falcon Heavy, world's most powerful rocket
Davido's "Fall" becomes longest-charting Nigerian pop song in US Billboard history
US PresidentDonald Trumpmakes presidential State of Union address to Congress a week later than usual due to government shutdown
Denis Villeneuve's science fiction film epic "Dune: Part Two" premieres in Mexico City, starringTimothée Chalametand an ensemble cast
Treaty of Waitangi is signed between 40 Māori chiefs (later signed by 500) and representatives of the British crown in Waitangi, New Zealand and shares sovereignty between the two groups
Spanish–American War ends, peace treaty ratified by Senate
France & Great-Britain sign accord over building channel tunnel
Daigo, 60th Emperor of Japan (897-930) and Buddhist monk, born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 930)
Džore Držić, Croatian poet and playwright who is considered one of the fathers of Croatian literature, born in Dubrovnik, Croatia (d. 1501)
Beatrice Cenci, Italian noblewoman executed for fratricide, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1599)
Antonio Vieira, Portuguese diplomat and preacher, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1697)
Antoine Arnauld, French Roman Catholic theologian and philosopher and mathematician, born in Paris, France (d. 1694)
Daniel Georg Morhof, German writer and scholar, born in Wismar, Germany (d. 1691)
Maximilian Dietrich Freisslich, German organist, composer, and Kapellmeister (Marienkirche (Danzig), 1715-31), baptized in Immelborn, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1731)
André Chéron, French composer and organist and harpsichordist, born in Paris, France (d. 1766)
Nicolaus II Bernoulli, Swiss mathematician, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1726)
Johann Matthias Leffloth, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1731)
Charles Lee, General in the American Revolution, born in Darnhall, United Kingdom (d. 1782)
Pierre-Joseph Desault, French anatomist & surgeon, born in Vouhenans, France (d. 1795)
Adam Weishaupt, German philosopher and founder of the Order of the Illuminati, born in Ingolstadt, Germany (d. 1811)
Évariste de Parny, French poet, born in Saint-Paul, France (d. 1814)
Julian Ursyn Niemcewicz, Polish scholar & statesman, born in Skoki, Poland (d. 1841)
Ugo Foscolo, Italian poet (Last Letters of Jacopo Ortis), born in Zakynthos Island, Greece (d. 1827) (26 Jan Greek calendar)
Pietro Rovelli, Italian composer, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1838)
Joseph Maria von Radowitz, German diplomat, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1912)
Charles Wheatstone, English scientist (sound transmission), and inventor (English concertina; stereoscope; Playfair cipher), born in Barnwood, England (d. 1875)
Hans Matthison-Hansen, Danish composer, born in Flensburg, Germany (d. 1890)
Henry George Liddell, English lexicographer & father of the Alice in Alice in Wonderland, born in Bishop Auckland, United Kingdom (d. 1898)
William Maxwell Evarts, American lawyer and statesman (Union), born in Charlestown, Massachusetts (d. 1901)
Charles T. Stork, Dutch entrepeneur and founder of cotton machine factory, born in Oldenzaal, Netherlands (d. 1895)[1]
Marcellus Monroe Crocker, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Franklin, Indiana (d. 1865)
John Brown Gordon, Major General (Confederate Army), born in Upson County, Georgia (d. 1904)
José María de Pereda, Spanish novelist, born in Polanco, Spain (d. 1906)
Edwin Klebs, German physician and bacteriologist (bacterial theory of infection, diphtheria bacillus), born in Königsberg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1913)
Ema Puksec, Croatian singer, born in Ogulin, Croatia (d. 1889)
William Dorsey Pender, Major General (Confederate Army), born in Edgecombe County, North Carolina (d. 1863)
Henry Irving [John Brodribb], British Victorian actor, 1st actor to receive a knighthood (knighted 1895) and the inspiration for Bram Stoker's Dracula, born in Bradford, England (d. 1905)
Yisrael Meir Kagan [Chofetz Chayim], Belarusian rabbi, born in Dzyatlava, Belarus (d. 1933)
Luigi Mancinelli, Italian orchestral composer, born in Orvieto, Italy (d. 1921)
Ignacij Klemenčič, Slovenian physicist, born in Trebnje, Slovenia (d. 1901)
Elias Disney, Canadian-American construction worker, entrepreneur, and father of Roy and Walt Disney, born in Bluevale, Canada West (d. 1941)
Eliza Johannes Meester, Dutch journalist and writer (Geertje), born in Harderwijk, Netherlands (d. 1931)
John Henry Mackay, Scottish-born German writer (Anarchists), born in Greenock, United Kingdom (d. 1933)
Karl Sapper, German traveller, explorer, and natural historian, born in Germany (d. 1945)
Syd Callaway, Australian cricket all-rounder (3 Tests, 6 wickets, BB 5/37, HS 41; NSW CA, Canterbury [NZ]), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1923)
Alfred Mombert, German poet, born in Karlsruhe, Germany (d. 1942)
Milton Bennett Medary, American architect (Washington Chapel), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1929)
Leonid Gobyato, Russian general and inventor (man-portable mortar), born in Taganrog, Russia (d. 1915)
Wilhelm August Schmidtbonn, German writer (Der Sprechende Eros), born in Bonn, Germany (d. 1952)
Waclaw Gieburowski, Polish conductor and composer, born in Bydgoszcz, Poland (d. 1943)
Carl Ramsauer, German physicist (Ramsauer-Townsend effect), born in Oldenburg, Germany (d. 1879)
Pedro Aguirre Cerda, Chilean educator, lawyer, and politician (President of Chile, 1938-41; Senator for Concepción, 1921-27), born in Pocuro, Chile (d. 1941)
Karl Weigl, Austrian-American pianist, composer (Apocalyptic Symphony), and educator, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1949)
E J "Tiger" Smith, English cricket wicket-keeper (11 Tests, 20 dismissals; Warwickshire CCC) and umpire (8 Tests 1933-38), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1979)
Josef Frings, German Archbishop of Cologne, born in Neuss, Germany (d. 1978)

(James) "Haven" Gillespie, American Tin Pan Alley era lyricist and songwriter ("That Lucky Old Sun", "Breezin' Along With The Breeze", "Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town"), born in Covington, Kentucky (d. 1975)
Ljudmil Stojanow, Bulgarian poet (Metsh i Slowo, Cholera), born in Kovachevitsa, Bulgaria (d. 1973)
Karel Wellens, Flemish painter, born in Lummen, Belgium (d. 1958)
Siegfried Kracauer, German writer, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1966)
William Parry Murphy, American physician and Nobel laureate, born in Wisconsin (d. 1987)
Muhammad Zafrulla Khan, President of UN General Assembly (1962-63), born in Sialkot, Pakistan (d. 1985)
Eric Partridge, New Zealand lexicographer, born in Waimata Valley, New Zealand (d. 1979)
María Teresa Vera, Cuban singer, guitarist and composer ("Veinte años"), born in Guanajay, Cuba (d. 1965)
Alberto [de Almeida] Cavalcanti, Brazilian director (Nicholas Nickleby), born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1982)
Ramon Novarro [Jose Samaniegos], Mexican-American actor (Ben-Hur, Mata Hari), born in Durango, Mexico (d. 1968)
Roy Smeck, American musician, born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 1994)
Ben Lyon, American actor (Hell's Angels, I Cover the Waterfront, Night Nurse), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1979)
George Brunies, American musician, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1974)
Louis Nizer, Jewish-American lawyer (defended blacklisted stars in '50s, Catspaw), born in London, England (d. 1994)
Claudio Arrau, Chilean pianist (Boston Symphony Orchestra), born in Chillán, Chile (d. 1991)
John Dunning, New Zealand cricketer (NZ medium-fast bowler of 30's), born in Omaha, New Zealand (d. 1971)
Pieter G Buckinx, Flemish writer (Temptation of Poverty), born in Kortessem, Belgium (d. 1987)
Władysław Gomułka, Polish communist politician, born in Krosno, Poland (d. 1982)
Amintore Fanfani, Italian politician (Prime Minister), born in Pieve Santo Stefano, Italy (d. 1999)
Edward Lansdale, United States Air Force Officer, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1987)
Israel Citkowitz, Polish-American composer (The Lambs), and pedagogue, born in Skierniewice, Poland (d. 1974)
Carlos Marcello, Tunisian-born gangster, born in Tunis, Tunisia (d. 1993)
George Poyser, English soccer defender (Brentford) and manager (Notts County, Manchester City), born in Stanton Hill, England (d. 1995)
Irmgard Keun, German author, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1982)
Dooley Briscoe MC, South African cricket batsman (2 Tests; Transvaal) and soldier (Military Cross 1941), born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 1941)
Eva Braun, German mistress and wife of Adolf Hitler, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1945)
John Lund, American actor (High Society, Wackiest Ship in the Army), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1992)
Forrest Towns, American athlete (Olympic gold 110m hurdles 1936), born in Fitzgerald, Georgia (d. 1991)
Thurl Ravenscroft, American voice actor (Tony the Tiger), and bass singer ("You're a Mean One, Mr. Grinch"), born in Norfolk, Nebraska (d. 2005)
John Crank, British mathematician, born in Hindley, United Kingdom (d. 2006)
Arthur Gold, Canadian pianist, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1990)
Liberato Firmino Sifonia, Italian composer, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1995)
Lothar-Günther Buchheim, German author, born in Weimar, Germany (d. 2007)
Lindsay Tuckett, South African cricket fast-medium bowler (9 Tests, 19 wickets, BB 5/68; Orange Free State), born in Durban, South Africa (d. 2016)
Louis Heren, English foreign correspondent and writer (China's Three Thousand Years), born in London (d. 1995)
James H. Scheuer, American politician (Rep-D-NY, 1965-73, 75- 93), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2005)
Bob Scott, New Zealand rugby union fullback (17 Tests; Auckland RU), born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 2012)
Denis Norden, British television personality, born in London (d. 2018)
Felicia Montealegre, Costa Rican-American actress (Studio One; Kraft Television Workshop), and social activist, born in San José, Costa Rica (d. 1978)
Patrick Macnee, British actor (The Avengers, Magnum, P.I., Sherlock Holmes), born in London, England (d. 2015)
Maurice Le Roux, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1992)
Billy Wright, English soccer defender (105 caps, captain x 90; Wolverhampton Wanderers 490 games) and manager (Arsenal 1962-66), born in Ironbridge, England (d. 1994)
Jin Yong [Louis Cha Leung-yung], Chinese Wuxia novelist, born in Haining, China (d. 2018)
Paolo Volponi, Italian communist and author (Road to Rome), born in Urbino, Italy (d. 1994)
Pramoedya Ananta Toer, Javanese author (Anak semua bangsa), born in Blora Regency, Indonesia (d. 2006)
Dale Long, American baseball infielder (MLB record 8 HRs in consecutive games 1956; Pittsburgh Pirates, Chicago Cubs), born in Springfield, Missouri (d. 1991)
Haskell Wexler, American cinematographer and director, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2015)
Sammy Nestico [Nistico], American Grammy Award-winning arranger (Count Basie Orchestra, 1968-84; Capitol Records), composer and trombonist, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2021)
Walker Edmiston, American character actor (Star Trek; Gunsmoke; Mission: Impossible; The Dukes of Hazzard), and puppeteer, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2007)
Smokey Burgess, American baseball player (Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Caroleen, North Carolina (d. 1991)
Oscar Sambrano Urdaneta, Venezuelan writer, born in Boconó, Venezuela (d. 2011)
Pierre Brice [Pierre-Louis Le Bris], French actor (Winnetou), born in Brest, France (d. 2015)
Sixten Jernberg, Swedish skier (Olympic gold 1956/60/64), born in Lima, Sweden (d. 2012)
Fred Trueman, England cricket fast bowler (England 67 Tests, 307 wickets), born in Stainton, England (d. 2006)
Mamie Van Doren, American actress, model, singer, and sex symbol (Teacher's Pet, The Navy vs. the Night Monsters), born in Rowena, South Dakota
Rip Torn [Elmore Rual Torn Jr], American actor, voice artist and comedian (Cross Creek, The Larry Sanders Show), born in Temple, Texas (d. 2019)
Camilo Cienfuegos, Cuban revolutionary, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1959)
Francois Truffaut, French director (Jules & Jim, Fahrenheit 451), born in Paris, France (d. 1984)
Leslie Crowther, English comedian and television presenter, born in West Bridgford, United Kingdom (d. 1996)
Walter Fauntroy, American pastor and politician (Rep-D-DC, 1971-91), born in Washington, D.C.
Vernon "Vern" Ehlers, American politician (US Representative from Michigan, 1993-2011), born in Pipestone, Minnesota (d. 2017)
J. Howard Marshall III, American businessman, born in San Francisco, California
Ellsworth Milburn, American composer and educator (Menil Antiphons), born in Greensburg, Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
Jair Rodrigues, Brazilian samba singer ("Disparada", "Majestade o Sabiáe"), born in Igarapava, Brazil (d. 2014)
